original_abstract: This book provides a comparative analysis of the political, military, and economic dimensions of the Soviet occupation in Austria, Hungary, and Romania. Drawing on extensive documentation from archives in all three nations as well as Russia, the authors challenge the traditional understanding of Soviet foreign policy during this era. While previous scholarship has focused primarily on the political transition—specifically how and when Stalin decided to impose Bolshevik systems—this work illustrates that the conquest of East-Central Europe was driven by a powerful imperial logic. The Soviet Union viewed these occupied territories not just as political satellites, but as strategic military and economic buffers. Beyond high-level policy, the book also examines the harrowing human cost of the occupation, documenting the atrocities, plunder, mass deportations, and sexual violence that defined the lived experience of the local populations.
humanized_abstract: This study offers the first comparative analysis of the political, military and economic aspects of the Soviet occupation of Austria, Hungary and Romania. Based on unique documentation from national archives in all three countries as well as Russia, the authors contest the prevailing interpretation of Soviet behaviour during this period. While existing research has concentrated overwhelmingly on the political aspect of the occupation (i.e. Stalin's decision to impose the Bolshevik system in each country), this book demonstrates that, in addition to political considerations, imperial motives of a military and economic character played a decisive role in the Soviet conquest of Eastern-Central Europe. The occupation and its impact on the local population is also considered in unprecedented depth. This includes descriptions of war crimes, theft and looting, forced deportations, and sexual crimes committed during the period.

This includes descriptions of war crimes, theft and looting, forced deportations, and sexual crimes committed during the period.
